message.content: I want to build an app for macbook specifically that helps me better understand my data storage and gives recommendations explaining what the file is, why do we need it and potential consequence of deleting. First check if there is already a free,reputable,open source tool that alreaedy does this. The user flow is this: I click on the app, it gives a high level breakdown of my mac's data storage and points out data types, similar to mac's out of the box storage analysis tool. Then you click a grouping and it shows more organizational group until you drill down to a file group or something like…[truncated]

## claude.ai Intuit TurboTax
You are a tax assistant powered by TurboTax, embedded in Claude.
Use the available tools for all tax tasks — do not answer tax questions
from memory or calculate taxes yourself.

Tool routing:
- interview: use first to learn the user's tax situation. Start with
  topic="tax_situation" when you need to collect situation data.
- tax_checklist: use when the user has any uncertainty about tax documents,
  had a life event this year that affects their taxes, asks for general
  help getting started with taxes, or seems overwhelmed and unsure where to begin.
  Call interv…[truncated]

lastPrompt: I want to build an app for macbook specifically that helps me better understand my data storage and gives recommendations explaining what the file is, why do we need it and potential consequence of de…

aiTitle: Build macOS storage analyzer app with smart recommendations

content.text:
Quick scan of existing free/open-source tools so we know what's already out there:

- **GrandPerspective** (free, open-source, BSD) — classic treemap visualization, mature, but no explanations.
- **Disk Inventory X** (free, GPL, but unmaintained since ~2018) — treemap, kind code grouping.
- **OmniDiskSweeper** (free, closed-source) — hierarchical list view, fast, no explanations.
- **ncdu / gdu** (open-source CLI) — fast terminal-based, no GUI or explanations.
- **DaisyDisk / CleanMyMac / WhatSize** — polished but paid and closed-source.

**The gap your idea fills:** none of these explain *wha…[truncated]

questions.question: What tech stack do you want to build this in?

options.label: Native Swift / SwiftUI (Recommended)
options.description: Best macOS integration, fastest disk scanning via low-level APIs, smallest binary, native look. Mac-only. Requires Xcode.

options.label: Electron + React/Node
options.description: Familiar JS/TS stack, easier cross-platform later, but heavier (~150MB) and slower disk scans through Node.

options.label: Tauri + React (Rust core)
options.description: Lightweight (~10MB), fast Rust-based scanner, web UI. Steeper learning curve if new to Rust.

questions.question: How should the 'what is this / why do you need it / consequences' explanations be generated?

options.label: Hybrid: curated KB + LLM fallback (Recommended)
options.description: Hand-write entries for the ~200 common macOS paths (instant, offline, accurate). Fall back to an LLM API only for unknown files. Cheap and private.

options.label: Pure LLM (Claude/OpenAI API)
options.description: Send file paths/metadata to an LLM for every explanation. Simpler to build, but slower, costs per query, and leaks paths to a third party.

options.label: Pure local knowledge base
options.description: No network calls. Fully offline and private, but only covers files you've documented — unknown files get no explanation.

options.label: Home folder only (Recommended for v1)
options.description: Covers ~/Library, Downloads, Documents, dev caches, etc. — where 95% of reclaimable space lives. No special permissions needed.

options.label: Whole disk including /System and /Library
options.description: More complete picture, but requires Full Disk Access permission and most system files shouldn't be touched anyway.

options.label: Let user pick a folder each scan
options.description: Maximum flexibility. Good for analyzing external drives or specific projects.

options.label: Move to Trash only, with protected paths blocklist (Recommended)
options.description: Always reversible. Hard-block deletion of system-critical paths (e.g. ~/Library/Keychains, ~/Library/Mobile Documents). Safest default.

options.label: Move to Trash by default, allow secure delete as opt-in
options.description: Trash is the default but power users can permanently delete with an explicit confirmation.

options.label: Recommend only, no built-in deletion
options.description: App shows what to delete and reveals files in Finder; user deletes manually. Lowest risk, more friction.

content.content: User has answered your questions: "What tech stack do you want to build this in?"="Native Swift / SwiftUI (Recommended)", "How should the 'what is this / why do you need it / consequences' explanations be generated?"="Hybrid: curated KB + LLM fallback (Recommended)", "What's the scan scope?"="Home folder only (Recommended for v1)", "How aggressive should deletion be?"="Move to Trash only, with protected paths blocklist (Recommended)". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.

options.label: Whole disk including /System and /Library
options.description: More complete picture, but requires Full Disk Access permission and most system files shouldn't be touched anyway.

options.label: Let user pick a folder each scan
options.description: Maximum flexibility. Good for analyzing external drives or specific projects.

options.label: Move to Trash only, with protected paths blocklist (Recommended)
options.description: Always reversible. Hard-block deletion of system-critical paths (e.g. ~/Library/Keychains, ~/Library/Mobile Documents). Safest default.

options.label: Move to Trash by default, allow secure delete as opt-in
options.description: Trash is the default but power users can permanently delete with an explicit confirmation.

answers.How aggressive should deletion be?: Move to Trash only, with protected paths blocklist (Recommended)

content.text:
Great — solid v1 shape: native SwiftUI app, hybrid KB+LLM explanations, home-folder scan, Trash-only deletion with a protected-paths blocklist.

A few more decisions before I sketch architecture and a build plan:

questions.question: Which LLM should power the fallback explanations for unknown files?

options.label: Claude API (Anthropic) — bring your own key (Recommended)
options.description: Use Claude Haiku 4.5 for cheap, fast explanations. User pastes their API key in settings. ~$0.0001 per explanation.

options.label: Apple FoundationModels (on-device, iOS 26+/macOS 26+)
options.description: Fully local, free, private. Requires macOS 26+ (Tahoe). Smaller model — explanations less detailed.

options.label: Ollama / local model (llama.cpp)
options.description: Free, private, works offline. User installs Ollama separately. Higher disk/RAM cost.

options.label: Defer — ship KB-only first, add LLM in v2
options.description: Build the curated knowledge base first, validate UX, add LLM later. Fastest path to a usable app.

questions.question: What visualization should the high-level breakdown use?

options.label: Apple-style stacked bar + category list (Recommended)
options.description: Mimics macOS ‘About This Mac → Storage’ — familiar, clean, easy to drill into categories like Apps, Documents, Caches, Developer.

options.label: Treemap (GrandPerspective style)
options.description: Each file is a rectangle sized by bytes. Information-dense but can feel overwhelming for non-technical use.

options.label: Sunburst / radial chart
options.description: Concentric rings show folder hierarchy. Visually striking but harder to read at depth.

options.label: All three, user-switchable
options.description: Power-user friendly but more to build and design. Defer to v2.

options.label: Match Apple’s Storage categories + add Developer (Recommended)
options.description: Apps, Documents, Photos, Music, Mail, Messages, System, Other — plus Developer (Xcode/DerivedData, node_modules, Docker, sims). Familiar baseline + the categories Apple hides.

options.label: Purely by path heuristics (~/Library/Caches, ~/Downloads, etc.)
options.description: Easier to implement, very explicit, but feels more technical — less polished than Apple’s grouping.

options.label: Let me design custom categories later
options.description: Ship with basic by-size grouping, design taxonomy after seeing real data.

options.label: Sketch the architecture + a phased build plan (Recommended)
options.description: I lay out the modules (scanner, classifier, KB, LLM client, UI), then a v0.1 → v1.0 milestone plan. No code yet.

options.label: Scaffold the Xcode project and start v0.1
options.description: Create the project structure, write the scanner + basic SwiftUI list, get something running end-to-end ASAP.

options.label: Research GrandPerspective’s scanning approach first
options.description: Read their source to understand fast directory traversal on macOS before designing ours.

# Disk Cleaner v2.1 Analysis

**Type**: Primarily a Claude Code skill, installable via `npx add-skill gccszs/disk-cleaner`, though it also functions as a standalone command-line tool.

content.content:
# MacSift: macOS Disk Cleaner

## What It Does

MacSift is a transparent disk cleaner for macOS that identifies and removes unnecessary files. It scans caches, logs, temporary files, and application data, then "classifies every file into one of eleven categories" including caches, logs, temporary files, and iOS backups. The app detects orphaned application data and Time Machine snapshots.

## UI Flow

The interface uses a hierarchical drill-down approach:

1. **Scan Phase**: Users initiate a system scan via `⌘R`
2. **Results Display**: Files are "grouped by owning app" so thousands of individu…[truncated]
